text.title: IP-Sensitive Workflow Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every IP-Sensitive Workflow Design application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Side-SaaS lives in personal GitHub org (not employer), personal cloud accounts, personal email. No shared accounts EVER.
rule.rationale: Even read-only access from employer accounts creates assignment ambiguity; full separation is the only defense.

rule.statement: Side-SaaS work happens outside employer hours, on personal devices, on personal networks. Documented via calendar + commit timestamps.
rule.rationale: Time-fencing is the IP-attribution evidence; commit timestamps + calendar create the paper trail.

rule.statement: Maintain a log: which tools, which time, which device, which network for each session. Sounds paranoid; courts have asked for it.
rule.rationale: Attribution log is the difference between summary judgment and trial; cost = 5 min/session, benefit = entire side-business.

rule.statement: Do not import code, designs, or knowledge from day-job into side-SaaS. If the same problem applies, re-derive from public sources.
rule.rationale: Knowledge transfer is the messy boundary; re-derivation creates a defensible paper trail.

rule.statement: Where employer requires disclosure of side projects, disclose in writing. Where not required, document the lack of requirement. Surprise is the enemy.
rule.rationale: Most employer-employee IP disputes are surprises; written disclosure (or written non-requirement) removes the surprise.
